revision.text:
'''Test Planet''' is a [[planet]] in the [[Solar System]].
== Description == Test Planet has a diameter of 12,000 kilometers. It orbits the [[Sun]] at a distance of 150 million kilometers. The planet has one natural [[satellite]], called Test Moon.
== Atmosphere == The atmosphere of Test Planet is composed primarily of [[nitrogen]] (78%) and [[oxygen]] (21%). This makes it suitable for various forms of life.
== History of observation == Test Planet was first observed by ancient astronomers. Modern telescopic observations began in the 17th century. Space probes have visited the planet multiple times since the 1960s.
== See also == * [[Solar System]] * [[Planet]]
== References == {{reflist}}
[[Category:Planets]]

In [[computing]], a '''widget''' is an element of a [[graphical user interface]] (GUI) that displays information or provides a specific way for a user to interact with the [[operating system]] or an [[application software|application]].
== Types == Widgets include: * [[Button (computing)|Buttons]] * [[Text box]]es * [[Scroll bar]]s * [[Menu (computing)|Menus]]
== History == The term ''widget'' in computing predates the modern GUI era. Early text-based interfaces had rudimentary widgets. The concept evolved significantly with the development of the [[Xerox Alto]] and later the [[Apple Macintosh]].
Modern widget toolkits include [[Qt (software)|Qt]], [[GTK]], and various web-based frameworks.
== Implementation == Widgets are typically implemented as objects in [[object-oriented programming]]. Each widget class encapsulates the rendering logic and event handling for a particular type of interface element.
== References == {{reflist}}
[[Category:Graphical user interface elements]]

'''Stub Article Example''' is a short topic.
{{stub}}

The '''history of tea''' spans thousands of years and involves many cultures across [[Asia]], [[Europe]], and the [[Americas]].
== Origins == Tea drinking likely originated in [[Yunnan]] province during the [[Shang dynasty]] as a medicinal drink. The earliest credible record of tea drinking dates to the 3rd century AD, in a medical text written by [[Hua Tuo]].
== Spread to East Asia == Tea was introduced to [[Japan]] by Japanese [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] monks who traveled to China. The practice of drinking tea became widespread among Japanese aristocracy during the [[Heian period]].
In [[Korea]], tea culture developed alongside Buddhist practices. The [[Goryeo dynasty]] period saw the greatest flourishing of Korean tea culture.
== European discovery == Portuguese priests and merchants were introduced to tea in China during the 16th century. The Dutch East India Company began importing tea into Europe commercially in the early 17th century.
== Modern era == Today, tea is the second most consumed beverage in the world after water. Global tea production exceeds 6 million tonnes annually. Major producing countries include China, India, Kenya, and Sri Lanka.
== References == {{reflist}}
[[Category:Tea]] [[Category:History of food and drink]]

'''Quantum computing''' is a type of [[computation]] that harnesses the collective properties of [[quantum mechanics|quantum mechanical]] phenomena, such as [[quantum superposition|superposition]], [[quantum interference|interference]], and [[quantum entanglement|entanglement]], to perform calculations.
== Principles == Unlike classical computers that use [[bit]]s (0 or 1), quantum computers use [[qubit]]s. A qubit can exist in a superposition of both states simultaneously, enabling quantum computers to process a vast number of possibilities at once.
== Hardware == Several approaches to building quantum computers exist: * '''Superconducting qubits''' — used by [[IBM]] and [[Google]] * '''Trapped ions''' — used by [[IonQ]] and [[Quantinuum]] * '''Photonic systems''' — used by [[Xanadu (company)|Xanadu]] * '''Topological qubits''' — being developed by [[Microsoft]]
== Applications == Quantum computing has potential applications in: * [[Cryptography]] and code-breaking * [[Drug discovery]] and molecular simulation * [[Optimization problem|Optimization]] problems * [[Machine learning]]
== Challenges == Current quantum computers face significant challenges including [[quantum decoherence|decoherence]], error rates, and the need for extremely low operating temperatures.
== References == {{reflist}}
[[Category:Quantum computing]] [[Category:Emerging technologies]]
